sound_type: loud overlapping calls -- colonial nesting colony; wauuuw calls, long calls, alarm calls in continuous colony chorus; louder and more assertive than Glaucous-winged colony; acoustic reflectance of rocky island habitat amplifies aggregate
ecosystem: Rocky island -- Pacific coast exposed rocky islands, offshore rocks, sea stacks; distinct from sheltered Salish Sea sites of Glaucous-winged Gull; more physically demanding exposure
season: Summer (breeding April-August; peak acoustic activity during territorial establishment and chick-rearing)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Pacific coast breeding population stable. Some regional variation in colony trends. Washington outer coast populations monitored. Common and abundant on Pacific coast.
milestone: Third Laridae entry at degree 291. Second colony soundscape in the arc. Pacific coast rocky island context introduced -- distinct from Salish Sea colony sites of Glaucous-winged (289). Western Gull is the hybrid partner of Glaucous-winged; together 289 and 291 document the Washington State large gull complex.

coordinates.description: Washington outer coast and Pacific coast south to Baja California. Washington breeding sites: Destruction Island, outer coast rocks. Oregon: Three Arch Rocks, other coast sites. California: Farallon Islands (largest colony), Año Nuevo Island. Puget Sound: winter presence, mostly hybrids.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Colonial nesting soundscape. Continuous overlapping chorus of wauuuw contact calls, territorial long calls, alarm calls, and chick begging calls.
acoustic_data.primary_call: Loud emphatic 'wauuuw' or 'keeow'. More assertive and louder than Glaucous-winged's mew call. Higher vocal output per bird per hour.
acoustic_data.long_call: Head-back extended descending series during territorial display. Frequent during active colony territorial confrontations. Multiple simultaneous long calls at peak activity.
acoustic_data.alarm_call: Rapid kek-kek-kek series. Triggered by aerial predators (Bald Eagle, Peregrine Falcon, Common Raven), human approach. Propagates through colony.
acoustic_data.acoustic_reflectance: Rocky island cliff faces and rock surfaces create acoustic reflectance that amplifies colony sound relative to open-water sites. Physical habitat contributes to E=7 rating.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7 vs. Glaucous-winged E=6: both colony soundscapes, but Western Gull is louder per bird (more assertive call character) and rocky island habitat amplifies the aggregate. E step is justified by species character and habitat.

nodes.title: Glaucous-winged / Western Hybrid Pair -- 289 and 291
nodes.description: Degrees 289 (Glaucous-winged) and 291 (Western) are the Laridae arc's internal pair: the two great large gulls of the Pacific Northwest. Hybrid zone documented at 289; 291 completes the pair. In Washington State: pure Glaucous-winged (Salish Sea colonies), pure Western (Pacific coast colonies), hybrids throughout intermediate zones. Together the two entries document the full Washington State large gull complex.

nodes.title: Rocky Island Colonial Nesting -- Pacific Coast Context
nodes.description: Western Gull colonies on exposed Pacific coast rocky islands: Destruction Island (WA), Three Arch Rocks (OR), Farallon Islands (CA). Cliff faces, steep slopes, flat cliff tops. Acoustic reflectance amplifies colony sound. Mixed species colonies: Brandt's Cormorant, Common Murre, Pigeon Guillemot alongside Western Gulls. More physically demanding environment than sheltered Salish Sea colony sites.

nodes.title: Pacific Coast Rocky Coast -- New Geographic Context
nodes.description: Western Gull introduces the outer Pacific coast as a geographic context to the SPS engine's Laridae arc. Distinct from Salish Sea / Puget Sound geography of many previous entries. Outer coast: exposed to Pacific swell, fog, wind. Washington's Pacific coast (Pacific County south) vs. Salish Sea (protected inland waters). Western Gull is the indicator species of this coast.

nodes.title: Named by Audubon -- Larus occidentalis, 1839
nodes.description: Named by John James Audubon in 1839. Occidentalis: western. The most literally directional name in the Laridae arc -- simply the western gull, the gull of the western coast. Audubon observed the species on the Pacific coast during his survey work. The name reflects the species' defining geographic character.

cross_references.relationship: E=8 + E=7 = E=15. Punk community sound (fast, raw, community-rooted) paired with colonial gull community sound (continuous, overlapping, colony-pressure driven). Both are what a community produces when doing what it must.

First Laridae E=6. Hybrid pair: Glaucous-winged (Salish Sea) + Western (Pacific coast). Together document full WA large gull complex. Hybrid zone connects the two species biologically and the two entries thematically.
Second Laridae E=7. Winter alarm event (290) separates the two colony soundscapes (289, 291) in the arc. Triggered alarm between ambient choruses.
Fourth Laridae E=7. Sandy spit. Tern sub-family. Arc closes. Energy holds at E=7 through arc's final entry.
